aligned_functional_domains.domain_name: Core JSON Data Model and Type System
aligned_functional_domains.justification: The C cJSON struct (with type enum, value union, linked list children) maps to Rust's algebraic JsonValue enum with specialized types (Object, Number, Short). The Rust version uses enum variants instead of a type-tagged struct, and separates concerns into dedicated files (value, object, number, short). The short string optimization in Rust replaces C's raw char* strings. Both provide equivalent creation, access, and manipulation APIs.

Both have dedicated parsing subsystems. C's parse_value/parse_array/parse_object/parse_string/parse_number map directly to Rust's Parser::parse() with internal methods. Both handle UTF-8, escape sequences, number parsing, and nested structures. The Rust version uses raw pointer-based buffer scanning similar to C's approach for performance. Error handling differs (C uses global_error pointer, Rust uses Result<JsonValue> with Error enum).

C's print functions (print_value/print_array/print_object/print_string/print_number) map to Rust's Generator trait pattern. C has 4 print modes (Print, PrintUnformatted, PrintBuffered, PrintPreallocated) which map to Rust's 4 Generator implementations (DumpGenerator, PrettyGenerator, WriterGenerator, PrettyWriterGenerator). Rust uses a trait-based approach for extensibility, while C uses function parameters (format flag, prebuffer size). Both handle string escaping and recursive serialization.

Both handle number parsing and formatting, but Rust uses a significantly more sophisticated approach. C's simple print_number/parse_number with get_decimal_point maps to Rust's Grisu2 algorithm with DiyFp high-precision arithmetic. Rust provides richer numeric conversion (as_f64, as_i64, as_fixed_point_u64, etc.) compared to C's basic double access. The functional equivalence is maintained despite different implementation strategies.

C uses configurable memory hooks (internal_malloc/free/realloc, cJSON_InitHooks) for allocation, while Rust relies on its ownership model and allocator. The Rust version implements short string optimization (Short struct) and inline key storage (Object's Key struct) which are performance optimizations not present in C. The functional equivalence is in resource lifecycle management, though the mechanisms differ fundamentally due to language paradigms.
evidence_signatures: cJSON_New_Item(hooks) -> JsonValue::new_object() / JsonValue::new_array() (allocation via Rust allocator)
cJSON_InitHooks(hooks) -> (no equivalent - Rust uses global allocator)

unmapped_source_nodes.reason: The entire JSON Utility Library (JSON Pointer RFC 6901, JSON Patch RFC 6902, JSON Merge Patch RFC 7386, object sorting) has no equivalent in the target Rust codebase. Functions like cJSONUtils_GetPointer, cJSONUtils_ApplyPatches, cJSONUtils_MergePatch, cJSONUtils_SortObject, and all their supporting functions are completely absent from the target RPG.

The testing and fuzzing infrastructure (test.c demonstration, fuzz_main.c harness, AFL harness, cjson_read_fuzzer.c) has no equivalent in the target Rust codebase. While the parser module contains some inline tests (it_should_parse_* functions), there is no dedicated test file, fuzzing harness, or AFL integration visible in the target RPG.

The public API header file (cJSON.h) has no direct equivalent. In Rust, the public API is defined through pub declarations and re-exports in lib.rs and mod.rs files, not through a separate header file. The functional content (type definitions, function declarations) is distributed across the Rust modules, but the concept of a standalone header file is absent due to Rust's module system.

Similar to cJSON.h, the utility header file has no equivalent because the entire utility library is missing from the target. Even if it existed, Rust would use module declarations rather than header files.
The JSON minification function (cJSON_Minify) which removes whitespace and comments from JSON text has no equivalent in the target Rust codebase.

The deep comparison function (cJSON_Compare) has no direct equivalent. While Rust implements PartialEq for JsonValue, the C version provides case-sensitive comparison options and recursive structural comparison that may not be fully replicated.
The global error pointer accessor has no equivalent. Rust uses Result<JsonValue> with Error enum for error handling instead of a global error state.
